narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51.6% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 33.50, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best forecasts bar exam success without considering gender or race. The three biggest influences on your prediction were your Year 1 decile ranking of 1, your LSAT score, and your Year 3 decile of 2—these stood out as having the strongest pull on the outcome. Most significantly, your low Year 1 decile pushed the model firmly toward predicting failure, as it placed you well below the typical 6th decile among students who passed the bar. Your Year 3 decile added to that concern, ranking much lower than the average 6th decile for successful examinees and reinforcing the risk signal. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it showed solid potential compared to lower scorers, even if below the 37.27 average for those who passed. Overall, your profile compares to admitted students by lagging in early academic deciles and standardized metrics, while your middle-income background aligns with about a third of passers but didn't outweigh the academic flags—unlike the upper-middle and high earners who dominate success stories. Full-time status matches the norm for 93% of those who passed, so it was neutral here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from bar prep programs tailored for full-time students aiming to excel early in law school. With targeted improvements in these areas, you could shift your predicted odds dramatically for future applications.
